sourceMovie.title: Pulp Fiction
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Pulp Fiction for the same reason it broke through in 1994: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a crime rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Quentin Tarantino keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The thriller and crime blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Tango & Cash
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Pulp Fiction clicked for you, Tango & Cash (1989) is a strong next stop: both trade in thriller and crime, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Reservoir Dogs
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Pulp Fiction clicked for you, Reservoir Dogs (1992) is a strong next stop: both trade in thriller and crime, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Pulp Fiction?
faq.answer: Try Tango & Cash, Reservoir Dogs, The Usual Suspects—each shares DNA with Pulp Fiction while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Pulp Fiction?
faq.answer: Pulp Fiction (1994) is best described as Thriller, Crime, Comedy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Pulp Fiction worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 8.5/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this thriller lane.
